title: Lookup Tables vs Equations for Shooter Tuning
subtitle: When robot physics is messy, a measured lookup table can beat a beautiful but incomplete equation.
summary: The robotics writing explains choosing lookup tables for shooter angle and RPM because real-world behavior was nonlinear and tunable.

artifact.title: Tuning curve response
artifact.description: The PID curve stands in for the closed-loop side; the page explains how lookup values choose the setpoint.

contentBlocks.text: The Equation Is Not the Robot

A projectile equation can estimate launch angle and speed, but an FRC shooter includes wheel compression, battery sag, spin, changing carpet interaction, mechanism flex, measurement noise, and game-piece variation. A lookup table records what the robot actually did.

contentBlocks.content:
MeasureTest shots at known distances.
StoreRecord angle and RPM that scored reliably.
InterpolateBlend between nearby distances.
RetuneUpdate values after mechanical changes.

sections.heading: What the Table Stores
body: A shooter table usually maps distance to mechanism setpoints: pivot angle, flywheel RPM, hood position, or spin differential. The drivetrain estimates distance; the table returns setpoints.
Interpolation is the bridge between measured distances. If 2.5 m and 3.0 m are measured, a 2.7 m shot can blend between them.

sections.heading: When Equations Still Help
body: Equations are still useful for initial guesses, simulation, sanity checks, and extrapolation warnings. The best system often combines physics intuition with measured calibration.
A table without physics can become dangerous outside its tested range; an equation without measurement can miss the robot's real behavior.

pitfalls: Forgetting to retune after changing wheels or compression.
Interpolating angles without checking actual shot arcs.
Blaming PID when the table setpoint is wrong.

quiz.question: Why might a lookup table outperform a projectile equation on a real robot?
quiz.explanation: Measured tables include real effects that simple equations often omit.
